case_name: M/S Crown Consultants (P) Ltd. vs The Oriental Insurance Company Ltd.
court: National Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission, New Delhi
facts: Complainant M/s Crown Consultants (a Mumbai private limited stock-broking member of the NSE) executed online trades on behalf of a new client M/s Sethia Investments and allegedly on behalf of a Mr. Nahata during 01-03 May 1996. After prices fell, the complainant squared up trades on 07.05.1996 and booked a net loss of approximately Rs. 43.05 lakh, which it claimed arose because it was deceived by an unidentified person impersonating the client. The complainant lodged a claim under an NSE-master insurance policy issued by The Oriental Insurance Company Ltd. (OP1) on 30.06.1996; OP1 appointed surveyors who recommended repudiation and OP1 repudiated the claim on 23.05.1997 citing delay, defective/member-constituent agreement and client-code recording issues. The complainant filed a consumer complaint seeking indemnity and other reliefs; the Commission heard evidence including affidavit from the NSE and the surveyor's report.
arguments_claimant: The complainant argued it had been deceived by an unknown caller impersonating its client (Sethia) when telephonic purchase orders were placed on 01-03.05.1996, that it filed its first information report/claim within 30 days of crystallisation/discovery (claimed as 01.06.1996), that the surveyor's report was biased and deficient, and that repudiation by OP1 was unjustified under the policy. It sought indemnification of Rs. 43,05,032/- (and broader reliefs totalling Rs. 66,30,500/- including interest and costs).
arguments_respondent: OP1 contended the complainant failed to exercise due diligence and breached NSE regulations (trading before executing member-constituent agreement, trading in the name of Nahata without separate agreement, not recording client details contemporaneously), that the claim was lodged after the 30-day period required by the Certificate of Insurance and that no timely FIR was lodged with police. OP1 relied on the surveyor's report and maintained repudiation was justified under the terms and conditions of the insurance policy.
ratio_decidendi: The Commission held that "the burden of proving that the loss was caused by a peril insured against is on the assured." Therefore, to obtain indemnity under "Section 1 (c) of the insurance policy" the assured must (a) have carried out the transactions "in accordance with the rules, regulations, bye-laws, etc., of the SEBI and/or the NSE" and (b) "establish that the loss was on account of the insured peril, i.e., it (the assured) was indeed deceived as to the identity of a person in carrying out the said transactions." The court further emphasized that a contract of insurance is based on the doctrine of uberrima fides ("utmost good faith") and that this duty "must pervade the entire range of conduct of both the assured/insured and the insurer all through the life of an insurance contract." Applying these principles, the Commission found the complainant failed to discharge the burden of proof and lacked the required good faith and compliance with exchange regulations, justifying the insurer's repudiation.
final_order: The Commission found "no deficiency in service on the part of the Opposite Party no. 1, Insurance Company in repudiating the complainants insurance claim" and dismissed the complaint as "totally devoid of merit or good faith."
summary: This is a consumer complaint by M/s Crown Consultants against The Oriental Insurance Co. for repudiation of an NSE-master insurance claim of about Rs. 43.05 lakh allegedly caused by deception in telephonic share orders on 01-03 May 1996. The legal issues were whether the insured proved the loss resulted from the insured peril under Section 1(c) of the policy and whether it complied with NSE rules and the policy's notice requirement. The Commission applied the burden-on-assured rule and the doctrine of utmost good faith, found procedural and credibility failings (delay, regulatory breaches, lack of due diligence) and dismissed the claim. Practical significance: insurers may validly repudiate where the insured cannot prove compliance with exchange rules, timely notice, and bona fide deception.
embedding_summary: M/s Crown Consultants (P) Ltd. v The Oriental Insurance Company Ltd., National Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission, Complaint Case No. 121 of 1999, decided 31st May 2011. Facts: NSE member executed large online trades for client M/s Sethia (and entries for Nahata) on 01-03 May 1996, squared up on 07.05.1996 with a loss ~Rs. 43.05 lakh; claim/intimation to insurer filed 30.06.1996; policy period 19.10.1995-31.05.1996; OP1 appointed surveyors under Section 64UM Insurance Act 1938 and repudiated on grounds of delay, unstamped/notarised member-constituent agreement and failure to enter client codes contemporaneously. Legal issues: burden of proof on assured to show loss due to insured peril (Section 1(c) of policy), duty of "uberrima fides" throughout life of contract, NSE Regulation 4.3.1 (member-constituent agreement), requirement to notify insurer within 30 days, and material non-disclosure/absence of due diligence. Outcome: complaint dismissed; no indemnity awarded. Key doctrines/citations: MacGillivray on Insurance Law; P.C. Chacko v LIC (2008) 1 SCC 321; United India Ins. Co. v Great Eastern Shipping (2007) 7 SCC 101; Ravneet Singh Bagga v KLM (2001) 1 SCC 66; policy clause PART I Section 1(c); consumer relief denied. Claim amount asserted ~Rs. 43.05 lakh; final relief denied.
legal_principles: Burden on the assured to prove the loss was caused by an insured peril (quoted: "the burden of proving that the loss was caused by a peril insured against is on the assured").

Assured must comply with applicable SEBI/NSE rules in executing transactions to claim indemnity (must "have carried out the transaction(s) ... in accordance with the rules, regulations, bye-laws, etc., of the SEBI and/or the NSE").

Insured must establish actual deception as to identity to trigger Section 1(c) coverage (must "establish that the loss was on account of the insured peril ... was indeed deceived as to the identity").
Doctrine of uberrima fides (utmost good faith) applies throughout the life of the insurance contract, including during claim presentation and investigation.
Failure to give timely notice (within the policy's stipulated 30 days) or to lodge/pursue appropriate police complaints can defeat an insurance claim.
Non-compliance with exchange procedures (member-constituent agreements, contemporaneous client identification/entry) and lack of due diligence may justify insurer repudiation.
An insurer's reliance on a surveyor's bona fide report does not necessarily constitute deficiency in service if repudiation is justified on facts.
sections_cited: Regulation 4.3.1, Chapter 4, Part A of the Capital Market Regulations of the National Stock Exchange (NSE)
PART I IN & OUT INDEMNITY Section 1(c) of the Insurance Policy (master policy Certificate of Insurance)
cases_cited: P. C. Chacko v Chairman, Life Insurance Corporation of India, (2008) 1 SCC 321
United India Insurance Co. Ltd. v Great Eastern Shipping Co. Ltd., (2007) 7 SCC 101
General Assurance Society Ltd. v Chandmull Jain, (1966) 3 SCR 500
United India Insurance Co. Ltd. v Pushpalaya Printers, (2004) 3 SCC 694
LIC of India v Smt. G.M. Channabasamma, (1991) 1 SCC 357
Ravneet Singh Bagga v KLM Royal Dutch Airlines, (2001) 1 SCC 66

National Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission, New Delhi 2011 Complainant M/s Crown Consultants (a Mumbai private limited stock-broking member of the NSE) executed online trades on behalf of a new client M/s Sethia Investments and allegedly on behalf of a Mr. Nahata during 01-03 May 1996. After prices fell, the complainant squared up trades on 07.05.1996 and booked a net loss of approximately Rs. 43.05 lakh, which it claimed arose because it was deceived by an unidentified person impersonating the client. The complainant filed a consumer complaint seeking indemnity and other reliefs; the Commission heard evidence including affidavit from the NSE and the surveyor's report. The complainant argued it had been deceived by an unknown caller impersonating its client (Sethia) when telephonic purchase orders were placed on 01-03.05.1996, that it filed its first information report/claim within 30 days of crystallisation/discovery (claimed as 01.06.1996), that the surveyor's report was biased and deficient, and that repudiation by OP1 was unjustified under the policy. OP1 contended the complainant failed to exercise due diligence and breached NSE regulations (trading before executing member-constituent agreement, trading in the name of Nahata without separate agreement, not recording client details contemporaneously), that the claim was lodged after the 30-day period required by the Certificate of Insurance and that no timely FIR was lodged with police. OP1 relied on the surveyor's report and maintained repudiation was justified under the terms and conditions of the insurance policy. The Commission held that "the burden of proving that the loss was caused by a peril insured against is on the assured." Therefore, to obtain indemnity under "Section 1 (c) of the insurance policy" the assured must (a) have carried out the transactions "in accordance with the rules, regulations, bye-laws, etc., of the SEBI and/or the NSE" and (b) "establish that the loss was on account of the insured peril, i.e., it (the assured) was indeed deceived as to the identity of a person in carrying out the said transactions." The court further emphasized that a contract of insurance is based on the doctrine of uberrima fides ("utmost good faith") and that this duty "must pervade the entire range of conduct of both the assured/insured and the insurer all through the life of an insurance contract." Applying these principles, the Commission found the complainant failed to discharge the burden of proof and lacked the required good faith and compliance with exchange regulations, justifying the insurer's repudiation. The Commission found "no deficiency in service on the part of the Opposite Party no. 1, Insurance Company in repudiating the complainants insurance claim" and dismissed the complaint as "totally devoid of merit or good faith." This is a consumer complaint by M/s Crown Consultants against The Oriental Insurance Co. for repudiation of an NSE-master insurance claim of about Rs. 43.05 lakh allegedly caused by deception in telephonic share orders on 01-03 May 1996. The legal issues were whether the insured proved the loss resulted from the insured peril under Section 1(c) of the policy and whether it complied with NSE rules and the policy's notice requirement. The Commission applied the burden-on-assured rule and the doctrine of utmost good faith, found procedural and credibility failings (delay, regulatory breaches, lack of due diligence) and dismissed the claim. Practical significance: insurers may validly repudiate where the insured cannot prove compliance with exchange rules, timely notice, and bona fide deception. Facts: NSE member executed large online trades for client M/s Sethia (and entries for Nahata) on 01-03 May 1996, squared up on 07.05.1996 with a loss ~Rs. 43.05 lakh; claim/intimation to insurer filed 30.06.1996; policy period 19.10.1995-31.05.1996; OP1 appointed surveyors under Section 64UM Insurance Act 1938 and repudiated on grounds of delay, unstamped/notarised member-constituent agreement and failure to enter client codes contemporaneously. Co. v Great Eastern Shipping (2007) 7 SCC 101; Ravneet Singh Bagga v KLM (2001) 1 SCC 66; policy clause PART I Section 1(c); consumer relief denied. Claim amount asserted ~Rs. 43.05 lakh; final relief denied. Burden on the assured to prove the loss was caused by an insured peril (quoted: "the burden of proving that the loss was caused by a peril insured against is on the assured"). Assured must comply with applicable SEBI/NSE rules in executing transactions to claim indemnity (must "have carried out the transaction(s) ... in accordance with the rules, regulations, bye-laws, etc., of the SEBI and/or the NSE"). Insured must establish actual deception as to identity to trigger Section 1(c) coverage (must "establish that the loss was on account of the insured peril ... was indeed deceived as to the identity"). Doctrine of uberrima fides (utmost good faith) applies throughout the life of the insurance contract, including during claim presentation and investigation. Non-compliance with exchange procedures (member-constituent agreements, contemporaneous client identification/entry) and lack of due diligence may justify insurer repudiation. An insurer's reliance on a surveyor's bona fide report does not necessarily constitute deficiency in service if repudiation is justified on facts.
